<findings>

[observation] [info] Isolated files — no import edges between 14 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
[observation] [info] No production entry points exist in this zone — tooling and CI can safely ignore it during dependency analysis.
[observation] [warning] Rex dogfoods its own PRD tooling by storing .rex/ state inside the package directory; this is architecturally intentional but the execution-log.jsonl committed to the repo should be gitignored to avoid noisy diffs.
[observation] [info] Zone contains heterogeneous artifacts (runtime logs, PNG images, markdown docs, JSON config) with no mutual imports — cohesion of 0 is expected and not a defect for a metadata zone.
[anti-pattern] [warning] rex package bundles .rex/ operational state (prd.json, execution-log.jsonl) in its directory with no confirmed .npmignore exclusion — publishing to npm would leak project-specific PRD data and execution history into the package artifact.
[suggestion] [warning] .rex/execution-log.jsonl is tracked by git and modified on every hench run, generating permanent diff noise in code review. Add it to .gitignore to prevent operational state from contaminating source history and reduce accidental staging risk.
[suggestion] [info] Zone "Rex Package Metadata" (rex-package-metadata) has at-risk risk (score: 0.50, cohesion: 0.00, coupling: 0.00) — approaching architectural risk thresholds

</findings>

<insights>

- Isolated files — no import edges between 14 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
- Zero cohesion and zero coupling indicate this zone is a grab-bag of unconnected artifacts (config JSON, execution logs, PNG images, README) with no import relationships — expected for a metadata/assets zone.
- The presence of .rex/ runtime state files inside the package itself shows rex dogfoods its own PRD tooling, which is useful for self-validation but risks conflating package source with operational state.
- No entry points means nothing in the broader codebase imports from this zone — it is purely passive data and documentation.
- Zone contains heterogeneous artifacts (runtime logs, PNG images, markdown docs, JSON config) with no mutual imports — cohesion of 0 is expected and not a defect for a metadata zone.
- Rex dogfoods its own PRD tooling by storing .rex/ state inside the package directory; this is architecturally intentional but the execution-log.jsonl committed to the repo should be gitignored to avoid noisy diffs.
- No production entry points exist in this zone — tooling and CI can safely ignore it during dependency analysis.
- The .rex/ directory (prd.json, execution-log.jsonl, workflow.md) lives inside the package directory without a visible .npmignore; npm publish would bundle operational project state into the registry artifact by default, potentially leaking internal PRD data and run history to anyone who installs the package.
- rex package bundles .rex/ operational state (prd.json, execution-log.jsonl) in its directory with no confirmed .npmignore exclusion — publishing to npm would leak project-specific PRD data and execution history into the package artifact.
- Every hench execution that dogfoods rex will mark .rex/execution-log.jsonl as a modified tracked file in git status, creating persistent diff noise. This noise increases the probability that operational state is accidentally staged alongside source changes during a commit.
- .rex/execution-log.jsonl is tracked by git and modified on every hench run, generating permanent diff noise in code review. Add it to .gitignore to prevent operational state from contaminating source history and reduce accidental staging risk.

</insights>
